note: The dramatic 1.2km entry gorge has been partly graded to a mostly firm surface and can be done with an electric cart or assistance, though there are uneven cobbled and sandy stretches. Carts for disabled visitors ease it; still rugged.

The iconic facade is reached at the end of the Siq on a sandy, mostly level forecourt; electric carts can bring visitors with disabilities to this point. Viewing is possible; approaching the interior/steps is not. The great achievable goal for limited mobility.
Reached only by a strenuous climb of roughly 800–900 rock-cut steps up the mountain; no accessible route whatsoever. Not suitable for wheelchairs, strollers or limited mobility.
A row of monumental tombs set above the valley, reached by uneven rock steps and slopes from the main trail; not accessible for wheels. Viewable from a distance across the valley.
The wide valley floor beyond the Treasury is sand and gravel with the carved facades and theatre alongside; firm-ish in stretches but uneven and long, hard for wheels without a cart.
The Roman-era street runs across the valley floor over uneven ancient paving and rubble; long, exposed and bumpy, difficult for wheels and tiring for seniors.
A ritual platform reached by a long steep staircase climb up the cliffs; no accessible route. Not suitable for limited mobility.
The candlelit walk down the Siq to the Treasury at night is atmospheric; the graded path helps but the darkness and uneven sandy stretches add difficulty. Possible with assistance to the Treasury.
The modern museum at the entrance is fully accessible with ramps, lifts and step-free galleries; a good accessible introduction before the site itself.
A smaller, quieter carved canyon nearby; flatter and shorter than main Petra but still with sand, rock and some steps into the chambers. Partly manageable in the entry stretch.
The town above the site has road-accessible viewpoints over the Petra basin reachable by car; a step-free way to take in the dramatic landscape without the trek.
